# Preface {.unnumbered}

I wanted to describe the world with equations.

That was not a sophisticated ambition — it came from undergraduate
calculus, from the moment I realized that a derivative was not just a
symbol but a rate of change, something real. I wanted to apply that to
business, to forecasting, to the kind of problems that actually matter
in practice. It took longer than I expected to find the right
vocabulary.

That vocabulary turned out to be optimization.

And optimization, pursued far enough, leads to machine learning — to
the realization that learning from data is, at its core, a problem of
minimizing a loss function subject to constraints. This book is my
attempt to build that path explicitly, starting from the first
principles of calculus and optimization and arriving at the
contributions of my PhD thesis: a unified family of Support Vector
Regression models that optimize percentage-error loss functions
directly.

## Who this book is for

This book is written for students who are comfortable with calculus and
linear algebra but have not yet seen optimization or machine learning
framed as a unified mathematical discipline. The early chapters require
nothing beyond multivariable calculus. The later chapters develop
graduate-level material, but the goal throughout is the same: to
motivate before formalizing, and to show the derivation rather than
just the result.

## How to use this book

Each chapter opens with a practical question or problem. The
mathematics are developed in response to that question. Advanced
derivations appear in collapsible callout blocks — they are there for
completeness and for the reader who wants every step, but skipping them
does not break the narrative. Code examples appear in paired R and
Python tabs.

## A note on notation

All notation is introduced when first used. A reference table appears
in the appendix.

## Acknowledgements

*To be written.*
